era_context: The French Revolution is in its early radical phase, with the monarchy under increasing pressure and the revolutionary government consolidating power. The War of the First Coalition (1792-1797) begins, with France fighting against a coalition of European monarchies. The Industrial Revolution is accelerating in Britain. The Americas are consolidating independence and establishing new republics. The Ottoman Empire is in decline, and Russia is expanding its influence.

events.title: Storming of the Tuileries Palace and fall of the French monarchy
events.description: On 10 August 1792, a mob of Parisians, supported by the National Guard and revolutionary forces, storms the Tuileries Palace, the residence of King Louis XVI. The storming of the palace marks the effective end of the constitutional monarchy in France and the beginning of the radical phase of the French Revolution. King Louis XVI and Queen Marie Antoinette are forced to flee the palace and are subsequently imprisoned. The storming of the Tuileries is followed by the September Massacres (2-7 September 1792), in which thousands of prisoners are killed by mobs. The storming of the Tuileries marks a turning point in the French Revolution, as the moderate constitutional monarchy gives way to a more radical republic.

sources.name: French government records and contemporary accounts
Personal correspondence and memoirs of revolutionaries

events.certainty_note: The date of the storming of the Tuileries Palace (10 August 1792) is well-established in French government records and contemporary sources. The event is one of the most thoroughly documented moments in the French Revolution.

events.title: September Massacres in Paris
events.description: On 2-7 September 1792, mobs of Parisians, in a state of revolutionary fervor and fear of counter-revolution, massacre thousands of prisoners held in Paris jails. The September Massacres result in the deaths of approximately 1,200-1,400 people, including priests, aristocrats, and common criminals. The massacres are motivated by fear of a royalist conspiracy and by revolutionary ideology. The September Massacres mark a turning point in the French Revolution, as the revolution becomes increasingly violent and radical. The massacres are condemned by many revolutionaries, but they are also defended by others as necessary acts of revolutionary justice.

sources.name: French government records and contemporary accounts
Personal correspondence and memoirs of revolutionaries

events.certainty_note: The dates of the September Massacres (2-7 September 1792) are well-established in French government records and contemporary sources. The event is thoroughly documented.
cross_references: Follows from 1792-001 (Storming of the Tuileries Palace)

events.title: Abolition of the French monarchy and establishment of the First Republic
events.description: On 21 September 1792, the National Convention formally abolishes the French monarchy and establishes the First Republic. The abolition of the monarchy marks the end of the constitutional monarchy phase of the French Revolution and the beginning of the republican phase. The First Republic is characterized by radical revolutionary policies, including the execution of the King (January 1793), the Reign of Terror (1793-1794), and the dechristianization campaign. The establishment of the First Republic is significant for establishing the principle of popular sovereignty and for demonstrating that a major European power could be transformed from a monarchy to a republic.

sources.name: Personal correspondence and memoirs of revolutionaries

events.certainty_note: The abolition of the monarchy on 21 September 1792 is well-established in National Convention records and contemporary sources. The date and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Follows from 1792-001 (Storming of the Tuileries Palace)
Led to 1793-001 (Execution of King Louis XVI)

events.title: Battle of Valmy: French victory in the War of the First Coalition
events.description: On 20 September 1792, French forces under General François Kellerman defeat a Prussian and Austrian army at the Battle of Valmy. The French victory is significant for establishing French military credibility and for preventing the invasion of France by the First Coalition. The battle is notable for the use of artillery and for the effectiveness of the French Revolutionary army. The French victory at Valmy demonstrates that the revolutionary government can defend itself militarily and boosts the morale of the revolutionary forces. The battle is followed by the French invasion of the Austrian Netherlands (present-day Belgium) and the beginning of French expansion.
events.certainty_note: The date of the Battle of Valmy (20 September 1792) is well-established in French and Prussian military records. The outcome and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797)
Led to 1792-005 (French invasion of the Austrian Netherlands)

events.title: French invasion of the Austrian Netherlands
events.description: In late 1792, following the French victory at Valmy, French forces under General Charles-François Dumouriez invade the Austrian Netherlands (present-day Belgium). The French forces defeat the Austrian army and occupy the region. The French invasion of the Austrian Netherlands marks the beginning of French expansion and the establishment of French control over the Low Countries. The invasion is followed by the incorporation of the Austrian Netherlands into the French Republic. The region remains under French control until 1815, when it is transferred to the Netherlands following the Congress of Vienna.
events.certainty_note: The French invasion of the Austrian Netherlands in late 1792 is well-documented in French and Austrian military records. The dates and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Led to 1793-004 (War of the First Coalition: French military successes)

events.title: War of the First Coalition begins
events.description: In 1792, the War of the First Coalition (1792-1797) begins, with France fighting against a coalition of European monarchies including Austria, Prussia, Britain, Spain, and others. The war is motivated by the desire of the European powers to suppress the French Revolution and to restore the French monarchy. The war is characterized by French military successes on land and British naval dominance at sea. The war lasts for five years and results in significant territorial changes in Europe, with France expanding its control over the Low Countries, Italy, and other regions.

sources.name: French government records and military dispatches

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the War of the First Coalition in 1792 is well-documented in French and coalition military records. The date and significance are uncontested.

events.title: Haitian Revolution: Continuation and escalation
events.description: In 1792, the Haitian Revolution continues to develop, with enslaved people and free people of color fighting for their freedom and independence. The revolution is characterized by complex alliances and conflicts between different factions, including enslaved people, free people of color, white colonists, and foreign powers (France, Spain, Britain). In 1792, the French National Assembly grants political rights to free people of color, which further escalates the conflict. The Haitian Revolution represents one of the most significant challenges to European colonialism and slavery in the Americas.
events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Haitian Revolution in 1792 is well-documented in French colonial records and contemporary accounts. The date and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Led to 1793-006 (Haitian Revolution: Continuation and consolidation)

events.title: Qing Dynasty: Qianlong Emperor's reign continues
events.description: In 1792, the Qianlong Emperor continues his reign over the Qing Dynasty. By 1792, Qianlong has ruled for 57 years and is in his 82nd year of life. The Qing Dynasty is at the height of its territorial extent, having conquered Tibet, Xinjiang, and Mongolia. However, by 1792, signs of decline are beginning to appear: corruption in the bureaucracy, the rise of the eunuch Heshen, and growing social unrest. The Qing economy is beginning to show signs of strain, and the population is growing faster than agricultural production.

sources.name: Qing imperial records (Qing Shilu) and court documents
Chinese historical chronicles and official histories
Contemporary European diplomatic reports from China

events.certainty_note: The continuation of Qianlong's reign in 1792 is well-documented in Qing imperial records. The date and circumstances are uncontested.

events.title: British East India Company consolidates control in India
events.description: By 1792, the British East India Company has consolidated its control over much of India, particularly in Bengal and the Madras Presidency. The Company's military victories over Indian rulers and French competitors have established British dominance. In 1792, the Company is expanding its administrative control and extracting revenue from Indian territories. The Company's rule is characterized by the subordination of Indian interests to British commercial interests and the extraction of wealth from India. The Company's expansion sets the stage for the formal British Raj, which will be established in 1858.

sources.name: British East India Company records and correspondence

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of British East India Company control in 1792 is well-documented in Company records and British government correspondence. The date and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader British expansion in India

events.title: Third Anglo-Mysore War ends
events.description: In 1792, the Third Anglo-Mysore War (1790-1792) ends with a British victory. The war results in the defeat of Tipu Sultan, the ruler of Mysore, and the loss of significant territory to the British East India Company. The Treaty of Seringapatam (1792) ends the war and establishes British dominance in southern India. The defeat of Tipu Sultan marks a significant step in British expansion in India and demonstrates the military superiority of the British East India Company over Indian rulers.

sources.name: British East India Company records and military dispatches

events.certainty_note: The end of the Third Anglo-Mysore War in 1792 is well-documented in British East India Company records and contemporary accounts. The date and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Led to 1794-012 (Tipu Sultan's resistance to British expansion)

events.title: Russo-Turkish War: Russian advances continue
events.description: In 1792, the Russo-Turkish War (1787-1792) ends with a Russian victory. The Treaty of Jassy (1792) ends the war and establishes Russian dominance in the Caucasus and the Black Sea region. The Russian victory results in the cession of significant Ottoman territories to Russia and the establishment of Russian control over the northern Caucasus. The Russian victory demonstrates the military superiority of Russia over the Ottoman Empire and sets the stage for further Russian expansion in the region.
key_figures: General Grigory Potemkin (deceased 1791, but his legacy continues)
events.certainty_note: The end of the Russo-Turkish War in 1792 is well-documented in Russian and Ottoman records. The date and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Led to 1796-008 (Russian advances in the Caucasus)

events.title: British naval dominance in the Caribbean
events.description: In 1792, British naval forces begin to dominate the Caribbean, capturing French colonies and disrupting French trade. The British naval dominance is reflected in the capture of French colonies such as Guadeloupe and Martinique. The British naval dominance in the Caribbean is part of the broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797) and reflects British naval superiority. The captures disrupt French trade and weaken French colonial power. However, many of these colonies are returned to France in subsequent peace treaties.

sources.name: British Admiralty and War Office records

events.certainty_note: British naval dominance in the Caribbean in 1792 is well-documented in British and French records. The dates and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797)

events.title: U.S. Presidential election of 1792
events.description: In 1792, the United States holds its second presidential election. President George Washington is re-elected for a second term, defeating challengers including Thomas Jefferson and others. Washington's re-election reflects the broad support for his leadership and the stability of the early republic. The election occurs during a period of relative peace and prosperity in the United States, though tensions are beginning to emerge between Federalists (favoring strong central government) and Democratic-Republicans (favoring agrarian interests and state power).

sources.name: Personal correspondence of Washington, Jefferson, Hamilton, and Madison

events.certainty_note: Electoral results and dates are thoroughly documented in official records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Mary Wollstonecraft publishes 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman'
events.description: In 1792, British writer Mary Wollstonecraft publishes 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman,' a groundbreaking work of feminist philosophy. The work argues for women's education, political rights, and independence from male domination. Wollstonecraft's work is significant for establishing the intellectual foundations of feminism and for challenging the prevailing assumptions about women's roles in society. The work is controversial and is criticized by many, but it becomes influential in shaping feminist thought and activism in subsequent centuries.

sources.name: Mary Wollstonecraft's 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman' (1792)

events.certainty_note: The publication of 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman' in 1792 is well-documented. The date and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader Enlightenment intellectual movement

events.title: Russo-Swedish War (Gustav III's War) ends
events.description: In 1792, the Russo-Swedish War (1788-1790, with renewed conflict in 1792) ends with the Treaty of Jassy (1792). The war results in the status quo ante bellum, with no significant territorial changes. However, the war demonstrates the military strength of Sweden under King Gustav III and the willingness of Sweden to resist Russian expansion. The war also demonstrates the limitations of Russian military power and the difficulty of expanding Russian territory against determined resistance.
events.certainty_note: The end of the Russo-Swedish War in 1792 is well-documented in Russian and Swedish records, though the exact date and terms vary slightly in different sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Russian expansion in the Baltic region

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed above have contested elements: (1) The storming of the Tuileries Palace is sometimes dated as 10 August 1792, but some sources cite different times depending on the calendar system used (Julian vs. Gregorian). The significance of the event is uncontested, but the exact date varies slightly. (2) The September Massacres are sometimes dated as occurring on 2-7 September 1792, but some sources cite different dates depending on the source. The fact of the massacres is uncontested, but the precise dates vary. (3) The Battle of Valmy is sometimes cited as a French victory, but some historians argue that the battle was more of a stalemate or a Prussian tactical victory despite the strategic French success. The significance of the battle in preventing the invasion of France is uncontested, but the tactical outcome is debated. (4) The Third Anglo-Mysore War is sometimes dated as ending in 1792, but some sources cite 1793 as the end date. The Treaty of Seringapatam was signed in 1792, but the war's formal conclusion is sometimes dated differently. (5) The Russo-Turkish War is sometimes dated as ending in 1792, but some sources cite 1791 as the end date. The Treaty of Jassy was signed in 1792, but the war's formal conclusion is sometimes dated differently.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1792 is rich and well-documented, particularly for Europe and the Americas. European sources (British, French, Spanish, Russian, Prussian) are abundant and detailed. Asian sources (Chinese, Indian) are also available but are often filtered through European interpretations. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history are less well-represented. There is a significant bias toward 'great men' and elite decision-makers; the experiences of ordinary people, enslaved persons, and marginalized groups are largely absent from the historical record. The French Revolution is extensively documented, but the sources often reflect the perspectives of the revolutionaries or their opponents rather than the experiences of ordinary people. Colonial-era scholarship on India often reflects European assumptions about 'progress' and 'civilization' that modern historians have critiqued. The sources for 1792 are generally reliable, but they reflect the perspectives and biases of their authors and the societies that produced them.

graph_edges.note: The storming of the Tuileries Palace led to the September Massacres
The September Massacres contributed to the abolition of the monarchy
The Battle of Valmy led to the French invasion of the Austrian Netherlands
The War of the First Coalition began with the Battle of Valmy
The end of the Third Anglo-Mysore War led to Tipu Sultan's continued resistance
The end of the Russo-Turkish War led to Russian advances in the Caucasus
